coredata.dc:title: Late-model N, B, and P-co-doped carbon dots as additives for friction-reduction and anti-wear
coredata.dc:creator: Zhu, Wei
Tan, Yuhui
Tang, Weiwei
Li, Yufeng
Tang, Yunzhi
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs), a kind of burgeoning carbon-based nanomaterial, were widely used as high-efficiency lubricant additives for promoting the friction-reduction and anti-wear behaviors of green PEG-based lubricating oils. Undoubtedly, advances in CDs-based lubricant additives play a crucial role in enhancing their application prospect and utility value. Recent studies suggest that doping CDs with heteroatoms, especially multiple heteroatoms could remarkably promote their tribological performance. Therefore, the state-of-the-art N, B, and P-co-doped CDs (N,B,P-CDs) with a diameter of approximately 3.8 nm and composed of amorphous structures were synthesized by conveniently “one-step” pyrolyzing the corresponding carbon sources and dopants. Noticeably, the synthetic procedures of N,B,P-CDs were uninvolved in any pre-processing and post-processing techniques, showing favorable large-scale preparation potential. Adding suitable concentrations of N,B,P-CDs, the friction-reduction, anti-wear, and load-supporting functions of PEG200 base oil can be maximally increased by 38.1 %, 86.2 %, and 66.7 %, respectively, showing outstanding tribological performances. In addition, the tribological behaviors of N,B,P-CDs are evidently better than that of N,B-CDs and P-CDs under identical test conditions, exhibiting a multiatomic synergistic lubrication effect. According to the wear track surface analysis results and previous reports, the excellent tribological performance of N,B,P-CDs attributes to their favorable film-forming ability and nano-lubrication effect in the different friction stages.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Polyatomic doping carbon dot
Lubricant additive
Polyethylene glycol
Friction-reduction
Anti-wear
Film-forming mechanism

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Dispersion stability of N,B,P-CDs in PEG200 base oil
Tribological property of N,B,P-CDs/PEG200 lubrication systems

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Natural Science Foundation of Sichuan Province
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Sichuan Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Sichuan Province [grant number 2023NSFSC0868 ].

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the synthesis procedures of N,B,P-CDs.
(a, b) TEM and high-resolution TEM (inset) images and (c) diameter distribution of N,B,P-CDs. (d) XRD patterns, (e) FTIR spectra, and TGA curves of N,B,P-CDs, P-CDs, and N,B-CDs.
(a) Full-scan and high-resolution (b) C1s, (c) O1s, (d) N1s, (e) B1s, and (e) P2p XPS spectra of N,B,P-CDs.

Photographs of the PEG200 base oil and 0.25 wt% of N,B,P-CDs/PEG200 dispersion as well as the diameter distributions of N,B,P-CDs in the dispersion (a, b) after preparation and (c, d) standing for two months.

(a) The COF curves of the PEG200 base oil and different concentrations of N,B,P-CDs/PEG200 dispersions. (b) The AFC and AWV of N,B,P-CDs/PEG200 dispersions as a function of concentration. (c) The COF curves of the PEG200 base oil, N,B-CDs/PEG200, P-CDs/PEG200, and N,B,P-CDs/PEG200 dispersions (0.25 wt%). (d) The AFC and AWV of the PEG200 base oil, N,B-CDs/PEG200, P-CDs/PEG200, and N,B,P-CDs/PEG200 dispersions (0.25 wt%). (Test conditions: 5 Hz, 20 min, 80 N, and room temperature).
(a) AFC and (b) AWV of the PEG200 base oil and 0.25 wt% of N,B,P-CDs/PEG200 dispersion as a function of load. (Test conditions: 5 Hz, 20 min, 80–800 N, and room temperature).

The 3D morphologies and 2D cross-section profiles of the wear track surfaces lubricated by the (a) PEG200 base oil, (b) N,B-CDs/PEG200, (c) P-CDs/PEG200, and (d) N,B,P-CDs/PEG200 dispersions (0.25 wt%). (Test conditions: 5 Hz, 20 min, 80 N, and room temperature).

(a) SEM image, (b) high-resolution (red rectangular area in SEM image) SEM image, and (c) elemental maps (blue rectangular area in SEM image) of the wear track surfaces lubricated by the PEG200 base oil. (d) SEM image, (e) high-resolution (red rectangular area in SEM image) SEM image, and (f) element maps (blue rectangular area in SEM image) of the wear track surfaces lubricated by the N,B,P-CDs/PEG200 dispersion (0.25 wt%). (Test conditions: 5 Hz, 20 min, 80 N, and room temperature). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

The high-resolution Fe2p, C1s, O1s, N1s, B1s, and P2p XPS spectra of the wear track surfaces lubricated by the N,B,P-CDs/PEG200 dispersion (0.25 wt%). (Test conditions: 5 Hz, 20 min, 80 N, and room temperature).
Schematic of the lubrication mechanism of N,B,P-CDs as additives for PEG200 base oil.
Elemental composition of N,B,P-CDs (Calculated (100 % - C% - O% - N% - B% - P%) for N,B,P-CDs).

Elemental compositions on the wear track surfaces of lower steel plates lubricated by the PEG200 base oil and N,B,P-CDs/PEG200 dispersion (0.25 wt%). (Test conditions: 5 Hz, 20 min, 80 N, and room temperature).

row.entry: Wear track surface lubricated by PEG200 base oil
Wear track surface lubricated by N,B,P-CDs/PEG200 dispersion

head.ce:title: Late-model N, B, and P-co-doped carbon dots as additives for friction-reduction and anti-wear

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangxi Provincial Key Laboratory of Functional Molecular Materials Chemistry, Department of Materials, Metallurgy and Chemistry, Jiangxi University of Science and Technology, Ganzhou 341000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangxi Provincial Key Laboratory of Functional Molecular Materials Chemistry
Department of Materials, Metallurgy and Chemistry
Jiangxi University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Jiangxi Provincial Key Laboratory of Functional Molecular Materials Chemistry, Department of Materials, Metallurgy and Chemistry, Jiangxi University of Science and Technology, Ganzhou 341000, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Biological and Chemical Engineering, Panzhihua University, Panzhihua 617000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Biological and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Biological and Chemical Engineering, Panzhihua University, Panzhihua 617000, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs), a kind of burgeoning carbon-based nanomaterial, were widely used as high-efficiency lubricant additives for promoting the friction-reduction and anti-wear behaviors of green PEG-based lubricating oils. Undoubtedly, advances in CDs-based lubricant additives play a crucial role in enhancing their application prospect and utility value. Recent studies suggest that doping CDs with heteroatoms, especially multiple heteroatoms could remarkably promote their tribological performance. Therefore, the state-of-the-art N, B, and P-co-doped CDs (N,B,P-CDs) with a diameter of approximately 3.8 nm and composed of amorphous structures were synthesized by conveniently “one-step” pyrolyzing the corresponding carbon sources and dopants. Noticeably, the synthetic procedures of N,B,P-CDs were uninvolved in any pre-processing and post-processing techniques, showing favorable large-scale preparation potential. Adding suitable concentrations of N,B,P-CDs, the friction-reduction, anti-wear, and load-supporting functions of PEG200 base oil can be maximally increased by 38.1 %, 86.2 %, and 66.7 %, respectively, showing outstanding tribological performances. In addition, the tribological behaviors of N,B,P-CDs are evidently better than that of N,B-CDs and P-CDs under identical test conditions, exhibiting a multiatomic synergistic lubrication effect. According to the wear track surface analysis results and previous reports, the excellent tribological performance of N,B,P-CDs attributes to their favorable film-forming ability and nano-lubrication effect in the different friction stages.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: With the forthcoming global energy crisis, improving energy efficiency seemed not just feasible, but imperative. Many researches have suggested that nearly 30 % of the world's energy is consumed to overcome friction and about 60 % of mechanical failures are caused by wear. The economic toll is induced by friction and wear more than tens of billions of dollars each year [1,2]. Pioneer workings have shown that advances in lubrication technology can effectively reduce the friction and wear of mechanical parts, promoting energy efficiency and decreasing mechanical failures, thereby significantly facilitating the sustainable development of human society [3,4]. Liquid lubricant, as the most common and widely used lubrication technology, has a history of thousands of years and briefly experienced four developmental stages (water, animal and plant oils, mineral oil, and synthetic oil) [5]. In the evolution of liquid lubricants, synthetic oils have become the most frequently-used lubricating base oil because of their phenomenal viscosity-temperature characteristics, high chemical inertness, outstanding noninflammability, wide application range, and excellent lubrication performance [6]. However, the overwhelming majority of synthetic oils are hard to degrade in the natural environment, resulting in their applications have been limited with people's consciousness of environmental protection increasing constantly [7,8].

Polyethylene glycol (PEG), a type of high-molecular polymer (molecular weight < 600), with excellent lubricity, degradability, and environmental friendliness, has been widely used as a lubricating base oil in liquid lubrication [9]. Nevertheless, the poor shear resistance, heat resistance, and oxidation resistance of PEG cause it easy to decompose under harsh working conditions (high contact pressure, temperature, and oxygen content), and then lose its lubricity [10]. With the development of lubrication technology, PEG-based lubricating oils are gradually difficult to meet the increasingly demanding performance requirements of modern mechanical equipment for lubricating oils. Numerous studies claim that a low dose of lubricant additives can greatly promote the lubrication performance of base oil, thus observably improving the energy utilization efficiency of mechanical equipment and extending its service life by hundreds or thousands of times [11–13]. Therefore, it is necessary to add a small number of additional lubricant additives, especially anti-friction and anti-wear additives to tune the lubrication performance of PEG-based lubricating oils [14–18]. It is generally accepted that lubricant additives are the essence of high-performance lubricating oils [19].

Carbon dots (CDs), a newfashioned carbon-based nanomaterial with a diameter of <10 nm, were serendipitously discovered in 2004 [20] and purposefully prepared in 2006 [21], have attracted considerable attention in biological [22,23], material [24–26], optical [27,28], and chemical [29–31] sciences because of their many unique properties [32,33]. Consequently, CDs were hailed as one of the most promising materials of the 21st century [34]. Meanwhile, CDs have exhibited a bright application prospect in the field of lubricant additive owing to their excellent physicochemical properties and abundant friction-reduction and anti-wear modes [35]. A large number of studies have shown that CDs as lubricant additives can promote significantly the lubrication performance of PEG-based lubricating oils [36–41]. For example, Wang et al. used small organic molecules as the precursors and imidazole ionic liquids as functionalized groups to successfully synthesize a series of CDs-based lubricant additives for PEG-based lubricating oils by pyrolysis, phase transfer, and anion exchange strategies. By integrating the film-lubrication of functionalized groups and nano-lubrication of the rigid cores of CDs, CDs-based lubricant additives could effectively improve the tribological performance of PEG-based lubricating oils [42–45]. Similarly, Mou et al. utilized amidation, atom transfer radical polymerization (ATRP), protonation, and anion exchange techniques to prepare various functionalized CDs. Adding a suitable concentration of CDs, the friction-reduction function of PEG-based lubricating oils can be increased by at least 30 %, and the increased amplitude of anti-wear behavior is >50 % [46–51]. Currently, the CDs used for improving the lubrication performance of PEG-based lubricating oils are mainly ionic liquids and their derivatives as well as small organic molecules functionalized CDs, which usually involve complicated pretreatment and after-treatment procedures, greatly increasing the production cost of CDs, and further limiting their commercialization prospect. In addition, the tribological performance of frequently used CDs-based lubricant additives still exists much room for improvement. Both theoretical and experimental results have suggested that doping CDs with heteroatoms (N, S, P, B, etc.) is an effective approach to promoting their tribological performance. The doped heteroatoms possess much higher reactivity and affinity toward the metallic friction surface than the traditional C and O, causing the heteroatom-doped CDs as lubricant additives usually exhibit better film-forming ability than those of undoped CDs [35]. Although heteroatoms doping can effectively improve the tribological performance of CDs-based lubricant additives, the relevant research involves exploiting atomic doped CDs-based lubricant additives, especially for improving the lubricity of PEG-based lubricating oils still in its infancy [12,39,52,53]. Multiple heteroatom co-doping was expected to further enhance the tribological performance, especially the friction-reduction and anti-wear of the CDs-based lubricant additives. Therefore, the exploitation of novel polyatomic (heteroatoms) doped CDs-based lubricant additives with simple preparation and great tribological behaviors is particularly important in promoting their application prospect and practical value.

In this work, the late-model CDs with doping by N, B, and P (N,B,P-CDs) were successfully synthesized by directly “one-step” pyrolyzing carbon source and dopants without any pre-processing and post-processing procedures. The geometrical morphology, crystal structure, elemental composition, and thermal stability of the prepared N,B,P-CDs were systematically investigated by various microscopic, spectroscopic, and thermal analysis techniques. The corresponding characterization techniques were provided in the supplementary material (SM). The lubrication behaviors of the N,B,P-CDs/PEG200 lubrication systems were evaluated by a typical UMT-3 tribometer. The impacts of the type of additive, additive concentration, and load on the friction-reduction and wear-resistance behaviors of the lubrication systems were revealed deeply. In addition, the convincing mechanism of action of N,B,P-CDs on the friction interfaces was revealed by combining various surface analysis techniques. The descriptions of tribological tests and wear track surface analyses are also shown in the SM.

Citric acid monohydrate (95.0 %, CA) was purchased from Tianjin Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (China). Phenylphosphonic acid (98.5 %, PPPA) and 3-Aminobenzeneboronic acid (95.0 %, ABBA) were gained from Energy Chemical Regent Co., Ltd. (China) and Beijing Macklin Chemical Regent Co., Ltd. (China), respectively. Ethyl alcohol (95.0 %) was obtained from Shanghai Aladin Chemical Regent Co., Ltd. (China). It is worth pointing out that all the chemicals were used throughout the experiment as received without further purification.

N,B,P-CDs were synthesized by directly pyrolyzing CA, PPPA, and ABBA under the specific conditions, and the schematic of the synthetic procedures of N,B,P-CDs is shown in Fig. 1 . Thereinto, CA was used as carbon source, PPPA and ABBA were utilized as dopants and reaction mediums. Briefly, 0.075 mol of PPPA and 0.075 mol of ABBA were consecutively added to a three-necked flask (50 mL), which was coupled with a reflux condenser. The flask was transferred to an oil bath and heated to 180 °C under magnetic stirring and an N2 atmosphere. When PPPA and ABBA were melted completely, 0.1 mol of CA was rapidly added to the flask and then maintained for 3 h. When the flask was cooled to the ambient temperature, the solid products were transferred to a drying oven and then maintained for 24 h at 80 °C. After drying, the obtained brown solid products are N,B, and P-co-doped CDs (abbreviated as N,B,P-CDs). It is noteworthy that the synthetic procedures of N,B,P-CDs are without any purification processes. Similarly, P-doped CDs (abbreviated as P-CDs) and N, B-doped CDs (abbreviated as N,B-CDs) were synthesized by severally pyrolyzing CA and PPPA as well as CA and ABBA under identical conditions.

Various concentrations of CDs/PEG200 lubrication systems were prepared by a traditional dilution method. Specifically, taking a certain weight of N,B,P-CDs was added to the fixed weight of PEG200 to prepare 1.0 wt% of N,B,P-CDs/PEG200 dispersion under ultrasonic treatment for 30 min. A series of different concentrations of N,B,P-CDs/PEG200 dispersions (0.05–0.8 wt%) were prepared by using PEG200 to dilute the above 1.0 wt% of N,B,P-CDs/PEG200 dispersion. Meanwhile, P-CDs/PEG200 and N,B-CDs/PEG200 dispersions were prepared by the same method.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was employed to analyze the geometrical morphology of CDs. The TEM images show that the synthesized N,B,P-CDs (Fig. 2 (a–b)), P-CDs (Fig. S1(a)), and N,B-CDs (Fig. S1(c)) have regular quasi-spheroidal shape and relatively uniform diameter distribution. Numerous studies have shown that CDs obtained by the pyrolysis method are usually composed of amorphous structures. There is no obvious lattice fringe in the high-resolution TEM images (insets in TEM images) can be observed, demonstrating that the above doped-CDs are mainly composed of amorphous structure, which can be further confirmed by the X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis. In Fig. 2(d), the XRD patterns of N,B,P-CDs, P-CDs, and N,B-CDs show the wide and short characteristic peaks centered at 20.9–23.3°, which can be attributed to the (002) plane of carbon-based materials, further verifying that the doped-CDs in this work mainly consisted of amorphous carbon. As shown in Fig. 2(c), Fig. S1(b), and Fig. S1(d), the results of the dynamic light scattering (DLS) characterization indicate that the average diameters of N,B,P-CDs, P-CDs, and N,B-CDs are approximately 3.8, 4.3, and 4.6 nm (<10.0 nm), respectively. These findings manifest that N,B,P-CDs with an amorphous structure and an average diameter of 3.8 nm were successfully prepared by the “one-step” pyrolysis approach.

Then, Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were used to investigate the surface groups of the doped-CDs and shown in Fig. 2(e). The characteristic peaks in the FTIR spectrum of N,B,P-CDs are located in 3680–3231, 1775–1650, 1625–1546, 1476–1343, 1299–964, 840–670 cm−1, assigning to the stretching vibrations of –OH and C=O/CO−NH, the asymmetric stretching vibration of CC, the deformation vibration of C−H, the stretching vibrations of C−B, the asymmetric stretching vibrations of C−O−C, and out-of-plane bending vibration of C−H (m-disubstituted benzene), respectively. Although the peak position appeared a slight shift, most of the above peaks can be observed in the FTIR spectra of P-CDs and N,B-CDs. In addition, the most of typical peaks in the FTIR spectra of ABBA and PPPA (Fig. S2) can be also found in the corresponding modified CDs. The TGA curves in Fig. 2(f) demonstrate that the residual mass of N,B,P-CDs, P-CDs, and N,B-CDs are approximately 38.9 %, 14.5 %, and 12.6 %, respectively, when the thermal decomposition temperature reaches 800 °C under the N2 atmosphere. Undoubtedly, the residue should be the undecomposed rigid carbon cores of CDs. This result indicates that the proportion of carbon cores of N,B,P-CDs is much more than that of P-CDs and N,B-CDs. Additionally, two obvious downward stages can be observed in the TGA curve of N,B,P-CDs. The weight loss at 100–168 °C can be ascribed to the evaporation of the absorbed water, while the weight loss located at 168–547 °C mainly originated from the thermal decomposition of the surface groups of N,B,P-CDs. However, the TGA curves of P-CDs, and N,B-CDs only show a downward stage at the temperature range of 75–326 °C, also attributing to the evaporation of the absorbed water and the thermal decomposition of the surface groups. The results of TGA characterization further verified that many PPPA and ABBA molecules were covalently grafted onto the surfaces of the corresponding CDs.

Subsequently, XPS analysis was performed to deeply reveal the element compositions and bonding states of N,B,P-CDs. As shown in Table 1 and Fig. 3(a), the full-scan XPS spectrum illustrates that N,B,P-CDs are mainly composed of C, O, N, B, and P, which composition formula can be expressed as C6.24O0.85N0.2B0.51P0.1. Fig. 3(b) exhibits that the fitting peaks of N–C=O/O–C=O (288.9 eV), C–O/C–N (286.6 eV), CC (284.8 eV), C–P/C–B (285.2 eV), and CC (284.4 eV) bonds can be observed in the high-resolution C1s XPS spectrum of N,B,P-CDs. In Fig. 3(c), the O1s XPS spectrum can be resolved into four peaks located at 533.7 (BO), 532.1 (CO), 531.9 (C=O), and 530.8 eV (PO). At the same time, the BO (190.6 eV) and PO (133.4 eV) bonds emerged in the high-resolution B1s (Fig. 3(e)) and P2p (Fig. 3(f)) XPS spectra, respectively. In addition to the above fitting peaks, the CN bond at 401.2 eV in Fig. 3(d), CB bond at 193.0 eV in Fig. 3(e), and CP bond at 132.7 eV in Fig. 3(f) can be severally detected in high-resolution N1s, B1s, as well as P2p XPS spectra. These results further confirmed that lots of N, B, and P were doped into N,B,P-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dispersion stability of N,B,P-CDs in PEG200 base oil
ce:section.ce:para: As a new-style CDs-based nano-lubricant additive, which dispersion stability in lubricating base oil determines its application prospect. Therefore, the dispersion stability of N,B,P-CDs in the PEG200 base oil was studied detailedly by the still-setting and DLS methods. Fig. 4(a) and (c) exhibit that there are no macroscopic agglomerates and sediments can be observed in the vessels whether the N,B,P-CDs/PEG200 dispersion (0.25 wt%) after preparation or was stored for two months, intuitively reflecting that the good dispersion stability of N,B,P-CDs in PEG200 base oil. Fig. 4(b) indicates that the average diameter of N,B,P-CDs is about 4.1 nm when the lubrication system was just prepared. Compared to the pristine N,B,P-CDs, the average diameter of N,B,P-CDs in the lubrication system is increased slightly. This case can be assigned to many PEG200 molecules that are adsorbed on the surfaces of N,B,P-CDs by van der Weals forces. Whereafter, the average diameter of N,B,P-CDs increased slowly with the increase of adsorbing capacity of PEG molecules until it reaches saturation. When the lubrication system was stored for two months, the average diameter of N,B,P-CDs only increased to 4.3 nm, with an increment of 0.2 nm. Clearly, the average diameter of N,B,P-CDs in the lubrication system has not happened that exponentially increase, confirming that N,B,P-CDs have not emerged microcosmic agglomeration during the storage. Similarly, Table S1 illustrates that the average diameters of P-CDs and N,B-CDs in the dispersions only a slight increase compared to the original P-CDs and N,B-CDs, whether the dispersions after preparation or were stood for two months. The above findings illustrated that N,B,P-CDs, P-CDs, and N,B-CDs possess good dispersion stability in PEG200 base oil, which created the prerequisite for its application in PEG-based lubricating oils.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Tribological property of N,B,P-CDs/PEG200 lubrication systems
ce:section.ce:para: Considering that the friction-reduction and anti-wear behaviors of the PEG-based lubricating oils are highly sensitive to the added concentration (wt%) of CDs-based lubricant additives. Therefore, the influence of added concentration of N,B,P-CDs on the lubrication behavior of the N,B,P-CDs/PEG200 lubrication systems was revealed thoroughly. As shown in Fig. 5(a), the friction coefficient (COF) curves of different concentrations of N,B,P-CDs/PEG dispersions are lower than the COF curve of PEG200 base oil. Obviously, adding 0.25 wt% of N,B,P-CDs in PEG200 base oil, the N,B,P-CDs/PEG200 dispersion shows the lowest COF curve, demonstrating the optical concentration of N,B,P-CDs in PEG200 base oil may be approximately 0.25 wt%. In Fig. 5(b), the average friction coefficient (AFC) and average wear volume (AWV) of different concentrations of N,B,P-CDs/PEG200 dispersions first decreased and then increased with increasing added concentration of N,B,P-CDs. Adding 0.25 wt% of N,B,P-CDs, both the AFC and AWV reached the lowest value. In detail, the AFC and AWV of the PEG200 base oil were reduced by 38.1 % and 86.2 %, respectively, when 0.25 wt% of N,B,P-CDs were added into it. Therefore, the 0.25 wt% of N,B,P-CDs/PEG200 dispersion shows the best friction-reduction and anti-wear performances in all the dispersions. Subsequently, to evaluate the effect of the doping atomic species on the tribological performances of CDs, P-CDs and N,B-CDs were also used as lubricant additives of PEG200 base oil. As shown in Fig. 5(c), comparing the COF curve of the PEG200 base oil, the COF curves of N,B-CDs/PEG200, P-CDs/PEG200, and N,B,P-CDs/PEG200 dispersions (0.25 wt%) are far lower than it. In Fig. 5(d), according to compare the AFC and AWV of PEG200 base oil, N,B-CDs/PEG200 dispersion, P-CDs/PEG200 dispersion, and N,B,P-CDs/PEG200 dispersion, the lubrication performance of the above lubrication systems is in the following order: N,B,P-CDs/PEG200 dispersion > N,B-CDs/PEG200 dispersion > P-CDs/PEG200 dispersion > > PEG200 base oil. These results demonstrated that the tribological behaviors of the polyatomic doped CDs should be closely related to the species of doping atoms, exhibiting a multiatomic synergistic lubrication effect. In addition, the kinematic viscosity of the above lubrication systems was also measured and shown in Table S2. The results indicate that their viscosity is very similar under the same temperature, reflecting that the viscosity of the lubrication systems has almost no effect on their lubrication performance in this work.

Subsequently, the load-supporting capacities of PEG200 base oil and N,B,P-CDs/PEG200 dispersion (0.25 wt%) were compared to evaluate the utility value of N,B,P-CDs as lubricant additives. As illustrated in Fig. 6 , when 0.25 wt% of N,B,P-CDs were added to PEG200 base oil, which load-supporting capacity can be increased from 480 N to at least 800 N, with an increased amplitude of >66.7 %. In Fig. 6(a), the AFC of PEG200 base oil and N,B,P-CDs/PEG200 dispersion decreased first and then increased with the increase of load within certain limits. Nevertheless, the variation trend of AMV of them exhibits a monotone-increasing function of load. These phenomena can be explained by the Hertz theory, which states that the contact area between the steel ball and the plate increases with the increase in load. Within a certain range of loads, the friction will reduce with increasing contact area, while wear is the opposite exactly. The above results demonstrated that N,B,P-CDs as lubricant additives can significantly improve the load-supporting capacity of PEG200 base oil.

As a brand-new CDs-based lubricant additive, comprehensively analyzing the morphology, geometric dimensioning, and elemental composition of the corresponding weak track surfaces by various surface analysis techniques (including the 3D optical microscope, SEM, EDS, and XPS characterizations) is indispensable for revealing its lubrication mechanism. Fig. 7 displays that the 3D morphologies and two-dimensional (2D) cross-section profiles of the wear track surfaces lubricated by the PEG200 base oil, N,B-CDs/PEG200, P-CDs/PEG200, and N,B,P-CDs/PEG200 dispersions (0.25 wt%) under identical friction test conditions. The size of the wear track lubricated by PEG200 base oil in Fig. 7(a) is much larger than the wear tracks lubricated by 0.25 wt% of N,B-CDs/PEG200 (Fig. 7(b)), P-CDs/PEG200 (Fig. 7(c)), and N,B,P-CDs/PEG200 dispersions (Fig. 7(d)). Fig. 7(a) shows the wear track lubricated by PEG200 base oil is extremely deep and wide, the depth and width are approximately 10.15 and 442.99 μm, respectively. When 0.25 wt% of N,B-CDs, P-CDs, and N,B,P-CDs were severally introduced into PEG200 base oil, the depth of wear track of it respectively declined to 3.70, 4.98, and 2.66 μm. The corresponding width of the wear track lubricated by PEG200 base oil is decreased to 269.25, 299.10, and 238.37 μm. Under the same test conditions, the deep and wide of the wear track lubricated by N,B,P-CDs/PEG200 dispersion are far less than the wear tracks lubricated by N,B-CDs/PEG200 and P-CDs/PEG200 dispersions. These findings not only intuitively suggested N,B,P-CDs as lubricant additives could significantly promote the anti-wear performance of the PEG200 base oil but also further confirmed that the anti-wear performance of N,B,P-CDs as the lubricant additives is superior to other CDs.

Fig. 8 gives SEM images, high-resolution SEM images, and elemental maps of the wear track surfaces lubricated by the PEG200 base oil and 0.25 wt% of N,B,P-CDs/PEG200 dispersion. In Fig. 8(a–b), many deep pits (marked by the yellow arrows) and furrows on the wear track surface lubricated by the PEG200 base oil can be clearly observed. When the wear track surface is lubricated by the PEG200 base oil, which easily decomposes under harsh working conditions (including high shear stress, contact pressure, and frictional heat), causing loses its lubricity. Owing to the decomposition of PEG200 base oil, resulting in the micro-convex on the friction surfaces encountered direct contact, thereby emerging many furrows and large-grained abrasive dust on the friction surfaces. The large-grained abrasive dust will cause serious abrasive wear, significantly aggravating the wear of the friction pairs. Nevertheless, as shown in Fig. 8(d), the wear track surface lubricated by 0.25 wt% of N,B,P-CDs/PEG200 dispersion is much smoother than that of the wear track lubricated by PEG200 base oil. Additionally, there are no obvious pits and furrows can be observed on the corresponding wear track surface. This finding further confirmed that the anti-wear performance of the PEG200 base oil can be enhanced significantly by introducing suitable concentrations of N,B,P-CDs. Surprisingly, many continuous and laminar films can be found on the wear track surface lubricated by the N,B,P-CDs/PEG200 dispersion (Fig. 8(e)). Fig. 8(f) and Table 2 show the above-mentioned films mainly consisted of Fe, C, O, N, B, and P, while the wear track surface lubricated by PEG200 base oil is mainly composed of Fe, C, and O (Fig. 8(c)). These results illustrated that the N,B,P-CDs participated in the formation of the films on the wear track surface lubricated by N,B,P-CDs/PEG200 dispersion, and the formed laminar films might be tribo-films.

To confirm the above results, XPS analysis was also performed to further study the elemental composition and chemical bonding state of the wear track surface lubricated by N,B,P-CDs/PEG200 dispersion (0.25 wt%). As illustrated in Fig. S3, the wear track surface lubricated by N,B,P-CDs/PEG200 dispersion is mainly made of Fe, C, O, N, B, and P, the result is consistent with the EDS characterization. Fig. 9(a) demonstrates the existence of Fe2O3 on the wear track surface, this finding can be further supported by the high-resolution O1s XPS spectrum. The fitting peak at 529.4 eV in Fig. 9(c) is attributed to the characteristic peak of metal oxides. As shown in Fig. 9(b), the fitting peaks 284.8, 285.2, 286.4, and 289.1 eV corresponded to CC, C–P/C–B, C–O/C–N, and metal carbonate bonds, respectively. In Fig. 9(c), in addition to the fitting peak of metal oxides, the fitting peaks of PO (530.9 eV), CO (532.3 eV), and CO (530.1 eV) bonds also can be detected in the O1s XPS spectrum. Additionally, Fig. 9(d) shows the N1s XPS spectrum can be resolved into two peaks centered at 399.8 and 402.0 eV, ascribing to the CN and oxynitrides. As shown in Fig. 9(e and f), the typical fitting peaks of CB (192.7 eV), BO (190.8 e), metal borate (190.2 eV), PO (133.6 eV), and CP (132.8 eV) bonds can be probed in the B1s and P2p XPS spectra. Compared to the high-resolution XPS spectra of N,B,P-CDs (Fig. 3) and wear track surface lubricated by N,B,P-CDs/PEG200 dispersion, although the peak positions appeared to shift slightly, the majority of characteristic peaks of N,B,P-CDs can be observed on the corresponding wear track surface. This finding suggests that many N,B,P-CDs with surviving in the tribochemical reactions embedded into the friction surface. To sum up, the complicated tribochemical reactions between the N,B,P-CDs/PEG200 dispersion and the metallic friction surface that happened during the friction test to establish the tribo-film, which is mainly composed of metal oxides, metal carbonate, oxynitrides, metal borate, and organic compounds (composed of C, O, N, B, and P), and N,B,P-CDs/PEG200. The formed tribo-film could efficiently prevent the friction surfaces from occurring in direct contact, greatly enhancing the friction-reduction and anti-wear behaviors of the N,B,P-CDs/PEG200 dispersion.

By summarizing previous reports and analyzing the experimental results of this work, the persuasive lubrication mechanism of N,B,P-CDs as lubricant additives for the PEG200 base oil was revealed first. When the metallic friction surfaces were lubricated by PEG200 base oil, which can be adsorbed on the friction surfaces by van der Waals' force. However, the PEG200 molecules will rapidly decompose under harsh working conditions (including high frictional temperature, contact pressure, and oxygen content), resulting in the micro-convex of metallic friction surfaces from happening direct contact. In this case, the friction pairs will emerge with serious friction and wear, thereby significantly weakening the friction-reduction and anti-wear functions of the PEG200 base oil. As shown in Fig. 10 , when suitable concentrations of N,B,P-CDs (0.25 wt%) were introduced into the PEG200 base oil, N,B,P-CDs relied on their ultra-small particle size could enter into the friction surfaces rapidly. At the same time, N,B,P-CDs on the friction surfaces can play the nano-lubrication effects (including rolling, mending, and polishing effects) to decrease the roughness and contact pressure of friction surfaces, reducing the friction and wear of the friction pairs in the initial stage of the friction test. As the friction test goes on, N,B,P-CDs and PEG200 molecules will take place the complicated tribochemical reactions with the substrates of metallic friction surfaces to establish the strong tribo-films under harsh working conditions. Then, the formed tribo-films mainly composed of metal oxides, metal carbonate, oxynitrides, metal borate, organic compounds, and N,B,P-CDs could efficiently prevent the friction surfaces from occurring direct contact, greatly reducing the friction and wear of the friction pairs. As a result, N,B,P-CDs as lubricant additives could signally promote the friction-reduction and anti-wear performances of the lubrication system, even under high load conditions.

The N, B, and P-co-doped CDs (N,B,P-CDs) with a molecular formula of C6.24O0.85N0.2B0.51P0.1 were successfully synthesized by a typical pyrolysis strategy without any pre-processing and post-processing procedures. The synthesized N,B,P-CDs possessed regular quasi-spheroidal shapes with an average diameter of approximately 3.8 nm and were mainly composed of amorphous structure. In addition, the surfaces of N,B,P-CDs were covalently modified by lots of PPPA and ABBA molecules, which endowed them with great compatibility with PEG200 base oil. In consequence, N,B,P-CDs can be directly used as the lubricant additives of the PEG200 base oil. As was expected, N,B,P-CDs have good dispersion stability in the PEG200 base oil. The friction test results demonstrated that the optimal added concentration of N,B,P-CDs is approximately 0.25 wt%. At this added concentration, the friction-reduction, anti-wear, and load-supporting performances of the PEG200 base oil can be increased by 38.1 %, 86.2 %, and 66.7 %, respectively, showing the best tribological behavior. Meanwhile, the tribological performances of N,B,P-CDs as lubricant additives are superior to those of N,B-CDs and P-CDs under identical test conditions, exhibiting a multiatomic synergistic lubrication effect. The excellent tribological performances of N,B,P-CDs are derived from their multiple lubrication effects in the different friction test stages. At the initial stage of the friction test, N,B,P-CDs in the lubrication system can quickly enter into the friction surfaces to play the nano-lubrication effects (including rolling, mending, and polishing effects), reducing the friction and wear of the friction pairs to some extent. Subsequently, the N,B,P-CDs/PEG200 lubrication system gradually reacted with the metallic friction surfaces to establish strong and complicated tribo-films. The formed tribo-films could long-time and efficiently prevent the friction surface from happening direct contact, reducing friction and wear of the friction pairs. Therefore, N,B,P-CDs as lubricant additives could significantly promote the friction-reduction and anti-wear performances of PEG200 base oil.

Wei Zhu: Formulation of overarching research goals and experimental scheme, English writing, Experimental test. Yuhui Tan: Conceptualization, Investigation. Weiwei Tang: Material characterization, English revision. Yufeng Li: Data collation and material characterization. Yunzhi Tang: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Sichuan Province [grant number 2023NSFSC0868].

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.diamond.2023.110315.
